1: שָׂפָה אַחַת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים (śāpāh ʾaḥat ûḏĕbārîm ʾăḥāḏîm) — "one language and common/unified speech." Two terms for language: śāpāh (lip, language — lit. the speech-producing organ, used as a metonymy for language) and ḏĕbārîm ʾăḥāḏîm (common/unified words — the same speech content, not just the same language). The pre-Babel condition was linguistic unity — a single system of meaning shared by all humanity. This will be the condition restored eschatologically (Zeph 3:9: "I will give peoples pure speech/lips").
2: בְּנָסְעָם מִקֶּדֶם (bĕnāsĕʿām miqqeḏem) — "as they journeyed eastward/from the east." The direction is ambiguous: either they moved east (Cain also went east, 4:16; Lot goes east toward Sodom, 13:11 — east = away from God in Genesis) or they came from the east. בִּקְעַת שִׁנְעָר (biqʿat šinʿār) — "the plain of Shinar," the same location as Nimrod's kingdom (10:10). The deliberate settling (wayyēšĕbû šām) reverses the command to fill the earth (1:28; 9:1) — they settle, concentrate, and build rather than scatter and fill.
3: הָבָה נִלְבְּנָה לְבֵנִים (hābāh nilbĕnāh lĕbēnîm) — "come, let us make bricks." The cohortative plural (three cohortatives in vv. 3-4: nilbĕnāh, nibĕneh, naʿăśeh) echoes and parodies the divine cohortative of 1:26 ("let us make mankind") — humans now say "let us make" in deliberate mimicry of the divine creative "let us." The building materials are noted: lĕbēnāh (brick, kiln-fired) instead of ʾeḇen (stone), and ḥēmār (bitumen/asphalt) instead of śîḏ (lime/mortar). The parenthetical observation contrasts Mesopotamian construction (brick and bitumen — the standard materials in the alluvial plain of Shinar, where stone is unavailable) with Palestinian/Canaanite construction (stone and lime). The narrator implicitly critiques the substitute materials: synthetic brick instead of natural stone, petroleum tar instead of lime.
4:
הָבָה נִבְנֶה-לָּנוּ עִיר וּמִגְדָּל — "come, let us build for ourselves a city and a tower." Lānû (for ourselves) — the self-referential purpose is emphasized: the construction is for their own benefit, consolidation, and glory. Migdāl (tower) — from gāḏal (to be great) — a great tower. וְרֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם — "with its top in the heavens" — the expression is idiomatic for extraordinary height (cf. Deut 1:28: "cities great and fortified up to heaven"; Dan 4:11: the tree reaching to heaven). It need not be taken literally as an attempt to climb to the divine realm.

וְנַעֲשֶׂה-לָּנוּ שֵׁם (wĕnaʿăśeh-lānû šēm) — "and let us make a name for ourselves." Šēm (name, reputation, fame) — this is the exact reversal of what God will do for Abram (12:2: "I will make your name great"). The builders seek to establish their own šēm through construction; God's plan is to establish the šēm of his chosen one through covenant. פֶּן-נָפוּץ (pen-nāpûṣ) — "lest we be scattered" — the very scattering they feared (and tried to prevent by city-building) is precisely what God brings about (v. 8). They resist the mandate to fill the earth (1:28; 9:1) by deliberately concentrating.
5: וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה לִרְאוֹת אֶת-הָעִיר וְאֶת-הַמִּגְדָּל — "and the LORD came down to see the city and the tower." The anthropomorphic irony is sharp: the tower built to reach the heavens requires God to "come down" to see it. The verb yāraḏ (to come down, descend) undercuts the tower's pretension — what the builders thought was heaven-reaching, the deity can only see by descending from his actual dwelling. The narrative's irony is literary theology: the human project is small enough that God must stoop to see it.
6: הֵן עַם אֶחָד וְשָׂפָה אַחַת לְכֻלָּם — "behold, one people and one language for all of them." God's assessment begins with what he sees: unity of language and people. וְזֶה הַחִלָּם לַעֲשׂוֹת — "and this is only the beginning of what they will do" — ḥillam (their beginning) = infinitive of ḥālal, to begin. לֹא-יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת — "nothing will be withheld from them that they purpose to do" — bāṣar (Niphal, to be withheld, cut off, inaccessible) — their unified capability poses a theological challenge: not that humans can actually reach God's heaven, but that human technological and political unity can amplify sin enormously. The divine response is not punishment for the tower itself but dispersal to limit the scope of unified human rebellion.
7: הָבָה נֵרְדָה וְנָבְלָה שָׁם שְׂפָתָם — "come, let us go down and confuse their language there." The divine cohortative hābāh nērĕḏāh (come, let us go down) directly mirrors and reverses the human cohortatives of vv. 3-4 (hābāh nilbĕnāh, hābāh nibĕneh-lānû): as the humans said "let us make" and "let us build," God says "let us go down." The divine response is structured as a counter-action to the human initiative, step by step. The "us" (nērĕḏāh, first-person plural cohortative) is the same plural as 1:26 — the divine council or the Trinitarian "us" — unexplained but deliberate.
8: וַיָּפֶץ יְהוָה אֹתָם מִשָּׁם עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ — "so the LORD scattered them from there over the face of all the earth." The verb pûṣ (Hiphil: to cause to be scattered, disperse) fulfills exactly what they feared (pen-nāpûṣ, v. 4). What they built the city to prevent, God brings about. The scattering is also the fulfillment of the original mandate: milĕʾû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (fill the earth, 1:28; 9:1) — God scatters them so the earth is filled as originally commanded, if not as they originally intended.
9: עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ בָּבֶל — "therefore its name is called Babel." The narrative etymology: bābel from Hebrew bālal (to confuse, mix — the verb used in v. 7: nābĕlāh śĕpātām, we will confuse their language). But the Akkadian name Bab-ilim means "gate of the god(s)" — the Babylonians understood their great city as the gateway between earth and heaven. The Hebrew narrator deconstructs this self-glorifying name: what the Babylonians called the "gate of God," the Bible calls "confusion." The wordplay subverts Babylon's entire self-understanding — its foundational claim to cosmic status is a pun on "confusion."
10: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת שֵׁם — "these are the generations/account of Shem." The tôlĕḏōt formula opens the post-Babel genealogy. This second Shem genealogy (compare 10:21-31) narrows from the broad Table of Nations to a single focused line: Shem → Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → Reu → Serug → Nahor → Terah → Abram. The precision of the genealogy (age at fathering, years remaining, total lifespan) matches the pre-flood genealogy of ch. 5, but lifespans decline sharply.
11: Shem's total lifespan: 100 years before Arphaxad + 500 years after = 600 years. Shem is the longest-lived of the post-flood patriarchs and, remarkably, outlives Abraham (who dies at 175, 25:7) — Shem was still alive during Abraham's lifetime by the genealogical arithmetic. The genealogy from Noah to Abraham spans approximately 10 generations, mirroring the antediluvian 10 generations from Adam to Noah (ch. 5).
12: Arphaxad's 35 years to fathering Shelah marks the beginning of the post-Babel genealogical line. The form of each entry (wayyiḥî X ben-N šānāh wayyôleḏ ʾet-Y, "X lived N years and fathered Y") uses the same waw-consecutive structure as ch. 5, creating a formal parallel between the antediluvian and post-Babel genealogies — two symmetrical spans between catastrophes (Flood; Babel) leading to the Patriarchal era.
13: Arphaxad's remaining 403 years after Shelah: total lifespan 438 years. The lifespan is already dramatically shorter than Shem's 600 — the post-Babel genealogy shows a systematic, generation-by-generation decline in longevity: Shem (600) → Arphaxad (438) → Shelah (433) → Eber (464) → Peleg (239) → Reu (239) → Serug (230) → Nahor (148) → Terah (205). The pattern is not perfectly linear but the trend is unmistakable: the antediluvian world (Adam to Noah: 930, 912, 905, 910, 895, 962, 365, 969, 777, 950 years) is giving way to the patriarchal world.
14: Shelah after 30 years fathers Eber — the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews. The brevity of the genealogical entries in vv. 12-25 reflects their structural-transitional function rather than narrative interest. Each entry confirms the unbroken chain from Shem to Abram, establishing the genealogical continuity of the covenant line across the post-Babel dispersion.
15: Shelah's additional 403 years: total 433. The regularity of the numerical pattern (30-35 years before fathering, then 200-430 years remaining) may reflect a schematic presentation rather than precise chronological record — as in ch. 5, the genealogy's theological function (continuity of the promise line) is primary; the numbers establish the bridge between the flood and the patriarchs.
16: Eber after 34 years fathers Peleg. עֵבֶר (ʿĒber) — the eponymous ancestor whose name gives "Hebrew" (ʿibrî). Eber is unusual in having the longest lifespan of the post-Babel patriarchs (464 years, v. 17), longer even than Shem (600 is longer, but Eber's 464 exceeds his grandson Peleg's 239 and all descendants through Terah). The longevity of the promise-line's most significant eponym may be deliberate emphasis.
17: Eber's additional 430 years: total 464. Eber is the last of the post-Babel patriarchs to live past 400 years. After Eber, the lifespans drop precipitously: Peleg (239), Reu (239), Serug (230), Nahor (148), Terah (205). This sudden drop in Peleg's generation corresponds to Peleg's name signifying the earth's division (10:25) — in the generation of the Babel dispersion, lifespans halve and the era of the patriarchs begins.
18: Peleg after 30 years fathers Reu. פֶּלֶג (Peleḡ) = "division" — his name marks the Babel generation. The genealogical line continues through Peleg (not Joktan) to reach Abram — the covenant line runs through the divided, dispersed world, not through the pre-division unity of Babel.
19: Peleg's remaining 209 years: total 239 years. The abrupt halving of the lifespan in Peleg's generation (from Eber's 464 to Peleg's 239) is the most dramatic transition in the post-Babel genealogy. Reu's lifespan (239 years, v. 21) matches Peleg's exactly — the two generations of Peleg and Reu mark the floor of the new lifespan era.
20: Reu after 32 years fathers Serug. The steady march of the genealogy moves the narrative toward Terah and Abram with increasing momentum — each generation brings the reader closer to the opening of the Abrahamic narrative in ch. 12. The genealogical bridge between the post-Babel world and the Patriarchal promises is being carefully constructed.
21: Reu's remaining 207 years: total 239 (matching Peleg's lifespan exactly). The genealogical precision continues without narrative commentary — the emphasis is on the unbroken chain of fatherhood from Shem to Abram, not on the individuals in the chain.
22: Serug after 30 years fathers Nahor. שְׂרוּג (Sĕrûḡ) — the name may be connected to the city Sarhugi/Serug in the Harran region (northwestern Mesopotamia), where Abraham's family will later settle (11:31-32). The geographical connection suggests the genealogy is also mapping the patriarchal family's homeland geography.
23: Serug's remaining 200 years: total 230. The progressive shortening continues: Serug (230) is shorter-lived than Reu (239) who is the same as Peleg (239). Nahor will live only 148 years (v. 25), the shortest lifespan in the post-Babel genealogy until Terah (205 = longer than Nahor but still in the new short-lifespan era).
24: Nahor after 29 years fathers Terah. נָחוֹר (Nāḥôr) — both the grandfather of Abram (here) and the brother of Abram (11:26-29) bear this name; the family naming pattern repeats names across generations, which is common in ANE genealogical practice. The Nahor of 11:22-25 is Abram's grandfather; Nahor in 11:26-29 is Abram's brother.
25: Nahor's remaining 119 years: total 148 — the shortest lifespan in the post-Babel genealogy. The dramatic decline from Shem's 600 to Nahor's 148 tracks across nine generations. The lifespan decline corresponds to the widening distance from the Flood (God's protective restraint of death) and the approaching era of the patriarchs, whose lifespans (Abraham 175, Isaac 180, Jacob 147, Joseph 110) are entirely within the human realm familiar to the ancient world.
26: וַיְחִי-תֶרַח שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת-אַבְרָם אֶת-נָחוֹר וְאֶת-הָרָן — "Terah lived seventy years and fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran." The narrative is on the verge of the Abrahamic promises. Note: Abram is listed first, though the chronology of 11:32 and 12:4 suggests he may not be the oldest son (if Terah was 70 when he fathered his first son, and Abram left Haran at 75 after Terah died at 205, Terah would have been 130 when Abram was born — meaning either Abram is the youngest of the three, or Abram is listed first by prominence rather than birth order, following the genealogical pattern of listing the significant son first).
27: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת תֶּרַח — "these are the generations/account of Terah." The final tôlĕḏōt formula before the Abrahamic narrative introduces Terah's family. The narrative now zooms from genealogical overview to dramatic close-up. הָרָן מֵת עַל-פְּנֵי תֶּרַח אָבִיו — "Haran died before/in the presence of (ʿal-pĕnê) his father Terah" — lit. "on the face of" = before the eyes of = while still alive. The premature death of Haran in Ur introduces the mortality theme that will haunt the Abrahamic narrative.
28: בְּאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתּוֹ בְּאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים — "in his land of birth, in Ur of the Chaldeans." The specification of Ur as the family origin grounds the patriarchal narrative geographically. Ur was one of the great Sumerian city-states of Mesopotamia (modern Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq), flourishing c. 2600-2000 BCE — a center of sophisticated urban civilization, astronomy, and the moon-god Nanna worship. The patriarchal family's origin in this cosmopolitan pagan city makes God's call to "leave your land" (12:1) a call out of the very center of the ancient world's civilized, urbanized order.
29: וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָם וְנָחוֹר לָהֶם נָשִׁים — "Abram and Nahor took wives." שָׁרַי (Śārāy) — Sarai's name is the variant form of śārāh (princess/noblewoman, which God will rename her in 17:15); the exact relationship between the two forms is uncertain — possibly a dialect or archaic variant. מִלְכָּה (Milkāh) — "queen" (from meleḵ, king) — the sister of Lot, Haran's daughter. The immediate family circle is tight: Abram marries Sarai (his half-sister, 20:12); Nahor marries Milcah his niece.
30: וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד — "now Sarai was barren; she had no child." ʿĂqārāh (barren) — from ʿāqar (to be barren, uprootedness). This single sentence creates the central tension of the entire Abrahamic narrative: the man God is about to promise "a great nation" (12:2) has a barren wife. The promise and the impossibility are placed in immediate juxtaposition — the covenant's fulfillment will require divine action beyond natural means. The matriarchal barrenness pattern (Sarai, Rebekah, Rachel; and Hannah, Manoah's wife in the later narrative) is introduced here as its inaugural instance.
31: וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן — "they went out with them from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to the land of Canaan." The journey to Canaan was Terah's initiative — he set out from Ur toward Canaan. But וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד-חָרָן וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם — "they came as far as Haran and settled there." The journey stopped at Haran (Ḥārrān in Akkadian, northwestern Mesopotamia, modern Harran in Turkey) — a city also associated with the moon-god worship and with the trade routes of the ANE. The incomplete journey creates the narrative gap that God's call of 12:1 will complete.
32: וַיָּמָת תֶּרַח בְּחָרָן — "and Terah died in Haran." The death of Terah closes the transitional genealogy and creates the narrative space for Abram's call. Terah's 205 years (unexpectedly longer than his son Nahor's 148) makes him an outlier in the declining lifespan pattern — possibly reflecting the narrator's theological interest in having Abram leave during his father's lifetime, or a traditional variant in the patriarchal genealogy. Stephen's account in Acts 7:4 states that Abraham left Haran after his father died — aligning with the sequence: Terah dies (11:32), then God calls Abram (12:1).
